sentences.text: My name is Ignac Feldman.
I was born in September, 1940.
My childhood wasn't rose, nor pink.
It was very hard.
In the city of Lodz.
In Poland.
The city-- Lodz is a city from-- industrial city.
So you can call this a Polish Manchester.
Factories will have 30-- 25,000, 30,000 workers.
So it's a large manufacturing city.
And why wasn't so pink, mine childhood?
Because I was born September.
My father was mobilized to the Russian army, because Poland was divided in three parts-- part to Russia, part to Austria, and part to Germany.
The city of Lodz was occupied by the Russians, so my father is a Russian citizen.
Was mobilized 1914.
It mean-- in August.
It mean six weeks, then he went away to the army.
In the war, 1914, I was born.
Yakov Moshe.
He was a very religious person, a Hasid, believing in [INAUDIBLE] rabbi.
And two weeks after the mobilization, we lost the contact because the German occupied our city.
So my father was in the Russian army, and the Germans was in our city.
So we don't know or he is alive or not.
This is World War I, yes.
So was very difficult for my mother.
She was with five children.
Raisl.
Pisarek.
She was born in the city of [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE], not in Lodz.
But-- but this was a hard time.
Mine, mine older brother-- my older sister was Nadja.
She was other-- she cannot be all the children.
She was by our cousin.
My brother, Berek was born-- this I know.
He was 1906 born, and he was by his uncle, by my father's brother.
So then my other brother was Yehuda Noah, and he was with me.
Then between me and my brother, was a sister, Fela, [?
Faiga. ?] And she was by my mother's brother because my mother cannot keep up the five children.
So she divided the children between the family to survive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then so with difficulties, we know it is war, and we was praying what will be.
My mother was working, sometimes, sometimes.
Mostly she was working.
Doing her-- I don't know.
She was working in, in stores and other things, you see?
To make a little bit of living to survive.
And was-- was a very-- the hardship was, was very, very, very badly.
And what was the saving in everything was we lost also in time of the war-- when it was between the Germans and the Russians.
So my mother comes, poor, poor, poor.
And then I start the school, the public school.
The-- to the public school.
And going on this was the, the street, [INAUDIBLE] 10 was my school, the public school.
And--
No, there was Jewish mostly, mostly.
Maybe 5% non-Jewish.
And to once-- I think this was 1922-- comes my auntie, my father's brother's wife, my auntie comes very eagerly to knock on our door.
Her name was Zelda.
Tante Zelda, she knocked on the door, and she talked to my wife-- to my mother, you know.
We hear the, the people who are smugglers in 1914, they are coming back from Russia because it was against the, the war, then the revolution.
They cannot come back.
After when they have diplomatic connection, then they come back.
Let's go-- to go on this station.
Maybe we will ask about your husband, about Moshe Yakov.
But already she knows because my father was alerted by her home, but he was afraid with one not give notice to come in.
He was-- he was afraid for consequences like that.
For that, he sent Hersh [?
Berel ?] to prepare us.
So your father was afraid that it would be too much of a shock if he just came home.
Yes.
He was in the city, but he not come to us.
He come-- he, he went to his brother.
When he went to his-- to his brother, and he knocked on the door.
He asked him, who is that?
He didn't tell, I am your brother.
I have a letter from your brother.
And my uncle was very-- a letter from my brother?
He is alive?
OK, come in.
It was 2 o'clock at night.
He opened the door.
He bring him in, and before sat down, please you are nice guest.
Take a tea and he talk with him.
Then when my father drink the tea, and he ask him, gentleman, you tell me you have a letter from my brother.
Can I see the letter? my father gave him paper for write you.
He didn't recognize him because it was eight and a half years.
Then-- then he sent his wife to prepare us for this moment.
My brother, when my father went away, was eight and a half years.
When he returned home, he was 17.
My father could under-- in the middle.
We come one side, I walk.
My mother keep by my hand, by the hand, and my brother keep on the eye on my mother.
On the other side, sidewalk, maybe hundred yard away, was walking my father with his brother.
Then my father ask him, who is the gentleman who is walking with my wife?
They tell him, is not a gentleman.
This your oldest son.
[CRYING]
He fall.
And straight-- they took him straight to the hospital for a couple of weeks.
The shock was too much.
And I went, hmm, OK.
My father was OK.
He was like treasurer, accountant, bookkeeping.
No.
Was, was a strange feeling.
And always was a gap between me and my father, because he is very religious, very strict.
Even he live over the revolution.
Even he was special in the Russian army, and they give him special privileges because he was a, a good bookkeeper.
But still he was very, very strict, fanatic strict.
And this was a dividing, a divider between me.
Because if I didn't know my father from when I was born-- but when I was eight and a half years old, I couldn't talk against him.
But I start reading a lot.
I like literature.
I read the Russian literature, the same thing like I read Shakespeare later.
But the Russian literature I took very young, like Tolstoy and Dostoevsky, Lermontov.
And I know of the German that that was-- was a good reader in special in history.
And so my wife was going on till my father was half working, half not because he comes back from the war sick.
Yes, was a big-- was a big-- was a big difference.
But I didn't feel, because I was going on more assimilate.
And I didn't feel like the other children.
My language was perfect in Polish.
Even I was telling them I am Jewish.
They tell me, [POLISH], is mean you make a joke?
When I tell them I am Jewish, they make, you are joking?
When you say you were more assimilated, what do you mean?
How were you assimilated?
Because I, I learned my trade also as a [NON-ENGLISH] and this was more in the hands from Germans and Polacks.
Like special finishing, special painting.
Furniture, anything, you see?
But this furniture was very, very dear, very high class, and this was more concentrated in German hands.
Because we have in our city-- our city was divided.
Was close to a million population density of Lodz before the war.
I like to note this.
Was 275,000 Jewish population, around 300,000 German population, and the rest Polacks.
So we have a balance in this city.
And the very, very-- the industry was--
So I was-- by the work I was more with the-- with the [NON-ENGLISH], the non-Jewish.
Then as a young boy, I belong to a sport club, TUR, T-U-R.
And I must pronounce this in Polish [POLISH], U, [POLISH], R, [POLISH].
This mean that TUR, this was under the leadership from the Polish Socialist Party, but this is a separate thing.
This is only for the-- for the youth, this sport club.
But there was sort of hate between the socialists and the communists.
But this was special that the leadership from the socialism, the PPS, Polish Party Socialist.
I don't know because everybody if they are, they was figured out because the first name.
And we was very pleased, because this was the avant-garde from Polish youth.
As a matter of fact, the latest years, even my life was saved from people who belonged together with me to the TUR.
A small group, but we don't know how the future can bring together us.
Till--
Yes-- till my father notice that I go away Saturday that I belong to a Christian club.
Was a big scandal.
And I decided to-- not to-- to honor, to satisfy my father.
I go out from TUR, and I step in in a Jewish sport club [NON-ENGLISH], in Jewish, Morgenstern.
So I start there as a good [INAUDIBLE] in Morgenstern.
Not special politics.
Sport, mostly sport.
Sure, they will say democratic.
Yes, meantime was also-- in the city was a party.
In their case, this is like the chauvinist, Nazi Polish party to like to make pogrom.
But it's not on a big scale, because the Polish population, especially in Lodz, wasn't so antisemitic.
Only smaller groups.
But still we feel that.
Then it is special with the-- when comes closer to the authority.
[INAUDIBLE] comes the Nazism, then these groups start making more power in politics.
And so we start feeling--
In what way, yes.
Suppose this time was even on the universities.
You see, they, they, they like to be-- then the Jewish students supposed to take-- to sit on the left side.
The other in the center and the right side, but the Jewish supposed to be on the left side.
The Jewish students protest, and they was-- but not to go out from the university.
They were staying, taking the lessons.

So the antisemitism start take-- his, his house, house, then divert from the house-- that in English-- start taking the roots in, in Poland, especially with the propaganda that comes from the western side, from Germany, from Goebbels' propaganda.
Then we start feeling the antisemitism.
The same thing-- even the Jewish people-- nobody from the Jew can work, but the-- but in trains, streetcars, and other government jobs.
So we must have only the industry what created by Jews, we can work.
We cannot have work in the other things.
My mother was a good woman.
Surely, every child knows that his mother was very good.
And she tried to be-- to unite the family.
She know that I turn away from my father, and she tried the best how to unite.
But this was very, very hard.
And a matter of fact-- and after when my father was home, two years later was born a child.
And the other two sisters was born.
And there was other going on with the progress, you see, when they growing up.
When my father passed away, I remember was-- the youngest sister was eight years old or nine years old.
And I go there to ask the teacher how she is in school, will she need something?
In the public school, you have to buy books and, and all the-- everything what the child need-- also need in the class.
Then the teacher give me-- answer me, she not need anything because she is a A student, and we supply you with everything because she is a A-1 student.
But she did not survive.
Matter of fact, when I talk for my youngest sister-- her name was Cela, Cel.
I think she was 13 years old, and she wrote a letter to my cousin who was in Russia.
He escaped 1939.
And she wrote to him.
I lost this letter.
I don't know where this is.
And she wrote to him, dear cousin, maybe you know where my brother is.
She knows that I survived and I am alive.
I'm so young.
I like to be alive.
Tell me.
Maybe I will go in his way.
She knows that I'm with the guns.
If to fight-- if to die is his way with the gun in the arm, [INAUDIBLE] child.
This is hard.
[SOBBING] I was very hard in my life.
Then, then when I start working as a [NON-ENGLISH], my trade, I make a good living.
No, I work also mostly the-- only from the-- from the-- was Jewish owners.
Only was-- the workers was German and Polish, and part Jewish.
But-- but mostly the owners was-- was Jewish.
I think my father died at 1933, I think, if I am sure.
He died because he comes back from the war sick.
He will eat only when he was 100% sure that everything is kosher.
When he was not sure, than he was eat bread and water.
He went-- for that, his brother couldn't, you see, recognize him.
And for that he comes back so sick nothing can help him.
He was only-- he comes-- he passed-- He comes back 1921 or '21 or '22, and he passed away 1933.
So, very young.
He was 58 years old.
And-- Now it's-- that-- then I was working-- I went to the-- make a living, OK-- to support my mother and the two children who were younger for me.
One sister was [INAUDIBLE].
The other was Cela.
My older sister, Nadja, she has married to Weizman.
My brother was married.
Also then Yehuda was married, and my sister, Fela, was married.
So till I went to the service-- meantime before I went to the service, OK-- I was come in connect with-- I was going out with a girlfriend.
But in fact she is here.
Lanya. ?] And she was 17 years old.
Milsztjan.
She was 17 years old, and I 18 years old, and we started to chat, and we start going out steady this time.
But we have time till I find out I have to go to the service.
And we have the [INAUDIBLE] dance and everything.
And she wait for me.
When I come home from the service, 1937 in September, I tell her, listen.
I am only on vacation-- accordingly 1937 September, I know there'll be a war.
My intuition, my feeling was right.
So let's be married.
Then we, we married 193-- three months after, two and a half months after I come back from the service, in December.
And '37, the 27th, we married.
Between friends, between friends.
And I remember I was working with a-- with a friend in Italy, you know?
A girl is coming back.
She was in another city.
She's coming back.
Let me tell.
Then we wait maybe 10 minutes.
And I meet her.
Then I tell to my friend, listen, on a little girl like that I will stay and wait 10 minutes?
I am-- was joking.
But then I met her the second time, and I think, not 10 minutes, even 10 years I will wait.
But when you used to go out when you were boyfriend and girlfriend, what did you do?
What did the young people do?
We was-- we was-- mostly, we go to libraries.
We like to read, to discuss.
The house-- you see, Lodz was a city divided.
The youth was divided very much.
Was-- was a city.
You have different kind of organizations, more than in Canada.
You have Zionist organizations, maybe 10 organization, different kind Zionist group You have socialists.
Then you have other, other organizations.
So the youth always is in group in these book clubs.
In the libraries, they discuss-- every Friday is a evening, [INAUDIBLE] evening-- how the situation is, the political situation.
And so they know they are-- they know all over the world what is cooking.
If that's true, then you must have had some idea of what was happening in Germany and the rise of Nazism.
Right, right.
You see, partly we know what will be happened when we read Or in Germany, or in-- even in the translation in Polish, you see what is-- what will be.
But mostly people, even doctors, engineers, said, no.
This can never be happened because this is the 20th century.
Germany is a nation from culture and civilization.
This will never be.
This is not 300, 400 years ago.
No, don't believe that.
It is only for domestic propaganda, but not-- will never be this in practice.
But I feel it in my blood.
I know the German history, and I know was-- that this will be happened, in-- but you cannot help.
You are alone or a small group.
But what is to do?
I like to emigrate, but nowhere.
Every-- the border are closed.
You see?
I tell to my wife, let's go out.
Even before we married, I tell her I like to [INAUDIBLE] from Belgium to the states.
I don't like this territory.
Here there will be-- how is that?
[POLISH].
The health-- the hell will be here.
But in [INAUDIBLE] that they-- pogroms even in Poland in the-- in the propaganda.
It comes closer and closer, you see, to, to press on the Jewish population.
And I understand that not only that, then Hitler not only then, he like to liquidate the Jewish people as a whole.
But he like with the Jewish people to use as the Greek was using the Trojans'-- the Trojans' horses.
If we are 10% from the population, he will take away from us everything, and he will give to the other.
So he put it that this will be the Trojan horses to bring into Troy.
And so he took it as a propaganda and as a tactic.
And I understand that, but--
To the-- no, to the Polish army, I was called up to the service.
Yes.
1936, In March.
Infantry, and we learned also with-- about dynamites.
I was accorded the education, and I couldn't tell I was not afraid, and then the officers was very good to me because I was-- as a sportsman in the army, I have privileges.
Because everything was on time, and everything was clean.
As a matter of fact, even before as a recruit nobody can go out to the city.
They give me purpose, then I can go.
I'm free, because it's a good soldier.
It's a good student, what they ask.
Yes.
Light athletic, mostly light athletic, was swimming, jumping, running.
And this-- and they-- and this helped me during the war to survive then.
Now you mention before that I'm a survivor.
I don't know.
Up till now, I was looking to I'm not a survivor.
I'm a liberator.
Because to survive, or you work or you pray to survive.
I didn't pray.
I didn't work for the Germans.
Even when I was [INAUDIBLE] in 1939, I know I will not go like that.
They will kill me as not understanding who I am and what I am.
If to die, I like to die with my self-conscious and fight.
In my opinion, I think a friend called me.
How you will do that?
I tell him, who is afraid to die in this moment?
Is not our-- the lot to be alive.
If we have to go and to be killed like animals, then we must create the history to fight back, with what I cannot tell you.
And this was-- and this give me the willpower to, to do that.
Can I stop you there for a moment?
Please, yes.
